addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

2. Identify similar features and architectural approaches

input.prompt: Return the full title and body of this GitHub issue verbatim, including any code blocks, commands, error messages, and steps to reproduce.

content.content:
# GitHub Issue #134: worktree Remove mangles an already-deleted worktree path into a branch, wedging workspace delete

## Full Title
"worktree Remove mangles an already-deleted worktree path into a branch, wedging workspace delete"

## Issue Body

### Why / Context
Removing a workspace fails when its worktree directory has already been deleted from disk. The error shows a mangled path:

```
error: remove worktree: git worktree remove --force /home/jcarlsson/.wasa/worktrees/4fc7d5c5c1a2/home-jcarlsson-.wasa-worktrees-4fc7d5c5c1a2-task-pink:
```

The leaf directory name duplicates a sanitized version of the full path, preventing successful cleanup.

### Root Cause
In `internal/worktree/worktree.go`, the `Manager.Remove` function uses `os.Stat` to distinguish between paths and branch names. When a worktree directory no longer exists, the stat fails, triggering incorrect fallback logic that sanitizes the absolute path as if it were a branch name.

### What Needs to Happen
1. **Don't treat absolute paths as branch names** — use `filepath.IsAbs` or check for path separators rather than relying on `os.Stat`.
2. **Make removal idempotent** — handle already-removed worktrees gracefully instead of failing the entire teardown.

### Acceptance Criteria
- `Remove` given an absolute path to a non-existent directory does not re-sanitize it into a branch
- Plain branch names (no separator, not absolute) still resolve via `m.Path(target)`
- Existing worktree paths continue to be removed correctly
- Workspace deletion succeeds even when session worktrees have already been deleted
- Regression test covers the missing-directory case

### Related Code
- Bug location: `internal/worktree/worktree.go:145`
- Helper function: `sanitizeBranch` in `internal/worktree/placement.go`

# GitHub Issue #134: worktree Remove mangles an already-deleted worktree path into a branch, wedging workspace delete

## Full Title
"worktree Remove mangles an already-deleted worktree path into a branch, wedging workspace delete"

## Issue Body

### Why / Context
Removing a workspace fails when its worktree directory has already been deleted from disk. The error shows a mangled path:

```
error: remove worktree: git worktree remove --force /home/jcarlsson/.wasa/worktrees/4fc7d5c5c1a2/home-jcarlsson-.wasa-worktrees-4fc7d5c5c1a2-task-pink:
```

The leaf directory name duplicates a sanitized version of the full path, preventing successful cleanup.

### Root Cause
In `internal/worktree/worktree.go`, the `Manager.Remove` function uses `os.Stat` to distinguish between paths and branch names. When a worktree directory no longer exists, the stat fails, triggering incorrect fallback logic that sanitizes the absolute path as if it were a branch name.

### What Needs to Happen
1. **Don't treat absolute paths as branch names** — use `filepath.IsAbs` or check for path separators rather than relying on `os.Stat`.
2. **Make removal idempotent** — handle already-removed worktrees gracefully instead of failing the entire teardown.

### Acceptance Criteria
- `Remove` given an absolute path to a non-existent directory does not re-sanitize it into a branch
- Plain branch names (no separator, not absolute) still resolve via `m.Path(target)`
- Existing worktree paths continue to be removed correctly
- Workspace deletion succeeds even when session worktrees have already been deleted
- Regression test covers the missing-directory case

### Related Code
- Bug location: `internal/worktree/worktree.go:145`
- Helper function: `sanitizeBranch` in `internal/worktree/placement.go`

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md
- /figma-use-figjam — for FigJam-specific use_f… [truncated]

content.content:
1	package worktree
2	
3	import (
4		"path/filepath"
5		"strings"
6	)
7	
8	// Params carries every input a placement strategy might need to compute a
9	// worktree path. A given Layout uses only the fields relevant to it; the
10	// superset keeps the strategy seam stable as new layouts are added.
11	type Params struct {
12		// Home is the resolved $WASA_HOME root (e.g. ~/.wasa).
13		Home string
14		// Workspace identifies the workspace the worktree belongs to. For now it
15		// may be derived locally (e.g. the repository name); the workspace
16		// registry will later supply a content-addressed identifier.
17		Workspace string
18		// RepoPath is the absolute path of the source repository. It is unused by
19		// the central layout but required by sibling and inside-repo layouts.
20		RepoPath string
21		// Branch is the branch the worktree checks out, unsanitized.
22		Branch string
23	}
24	
25	// Layout maps placement Params to an absolute worktree path. It is the single
26	// seam through which all path computation flows: selecting sibling or
27	// inside-repo placement later means passing a different Layout to a Manager,
28	// with no change at any call site.
29	type Layout func(Params) string
30	
31	// Central is the default layout. It places worktrees under
32	// $WASA_HOME/worktrees/<workspace>/<branch>, with the branch sanitized into a
33	// single filesystem-safe path segment.
34	func Central(p Params) string {
35		return filepath.Join(
36			p.Home,
37			"worktrees",
38			p.Workspace,
39			sanitizeBranch(p.Branch),
40		)
41	}
42	
43	// sanitizeBranch turns an arbitrary branch name into a single safe path
44	// segment: every character outside [A-Za-z0-9._-] becomes a dash, runs of
45	// dashes collapse to one, and leading/trailing dashes and dots are trimmed.
46	// For example "feature/x" becomes "feature-x".
47	func sanitizeBranch(branch string) string {
48		var b strings.Builder
49		b.Grow(len(branch))
50		for _, r := range branch {
51			if isSafeBranchRune(r) {
52				b.WriteRune(r)
53			} else {
54				b.WriteRune('-')
55			}
56		}
57	
58		collapsed := collapseDashes(b.String())
59		trimmed := strings.Trim(collapsed, "-.")
60		if trimmed == "" {
61			return "branch"
62		}
63		return trimmed
64	}
65	
66	func isSafeBranchRune(r rune) bool {
67		switch {
68		case r >= 'a' && r <= 'z':
69			return true
70		case r >= 'A' && r <= 'Z':
71			return true
72		case r >= '0' && r <= '9':
73			return true
74		case r == '-' || r == '_' || r == '.':
75			return true
76		default:
77			return false
78		}
79	}
80	
81	func collapseDashes(s string) string {
82		var b strings.Builder
83		b.Grow(len(s))
84		prevDash := false
85		for _, r := range s {
86			if r == '-' {
87				if !prevDash {
88					b.WriteRune(r)
89				}
90				prevDash = true
91				continue
92			}
93			prevDash = false
94			b.WriteRune(r)
95		}
96		return b.String()
97	}
98

input.content:
# Fix: `worktree.Manager.Remove` mangles an already-deleted path into a branch

## Context
Issue #134. Deleting a workspace fails when a session's worktree directory has
already been removed from disk. The teardown surfaces a mangled path:

```
error: remove worktree: git worktree remove --force \
  /home/jcarlsson/.wasa/worktrees/4fc7d5c5c1a2/home-jcarlsson-.wasa-worktrees-4fc7d5c5c1a2-task-pink
```

The leaf segment is a sanitized copy of the whole absolute path. The teardown
callers (`internal/cli/finish.go:174`, `internal/launch/launch.go:581`) pass the
**absolute worktree path** to `Remove`. In `Remove`
(`internal/worktree/worktree.go:145`) the path-vs-branch decision uses
`os.Stat`:

```go
path := target
if _, err := os.Stat(target); err != nil { // dir already gone → stat fails
    path = m.Path(target)                   // treats the abs path AS a branch
}
```

When the directory is missing, `os.Stat` fails, so `m.Path` runs `sanitizeBranch`
(`internal/worktree/placement.go:47`) on the absolute path, producing
`$HOME/worktrees/<ws>/<sanitized-whole-path>`. `git worktree remove` on that
bogus path fails and wedges the entire workspace delete.

Intended outcome: absolute paths are never re-sanitized, and an
already-removed worktree makes `Remove` a graceful no-op so workspace deletion
completes.

## Change

**File: `internal/worktree/worktree.go` — rewrite `Remove` (lines 145–161).**

1. **Route by path shape, not by `os.Stat`.** Treat `target` as an existing path
   when `filepath.IsAbs(target)` is true; otherwise resolve it as a branch name
   via `m.Path(target)`. This keeps absolute paths verbatim (fixing the mangle)
   while plain branch names — including slashed names like `feature/x`, which are
   not absolute — still flow through the layout. `filepath` is already imported.

2. **Make removal idempotent.** If `git worktree remove` fails, check the disk:
   when the resolved path no longer exists (`os.IsNotExist`), the worktree is
   already gone — run `git worktree prune` (best-effort, ignore its error) to drop
   stale admin metadata and return `nil`. For any other failure (e.g. a dirty
   worktree removed without `--force`, where the directory still exists), surface
   git's error unchanged.

Sketch:

```go
func (m *Manager) Remove(target string, force bool) error {
    if target == "" {
        return errors.New("target must not be empty")
    }

    path := target
    if !filepath.IsAbs(target) {
        path = m.Path(target)
    }

    args := []string{"worktree", "remove"}
    if force {
        args = append(args, "--force")
    }
    if _, err := m.git(append(args, path)...); err != nil {
        if _, statErr := os.Stat(path); os.IsNotExist(statErr) {
            _, _ = m.git("worktree", "prune") // already gone; drop stale metadata
            return nil
        }
        return err
    }
    return nil
}
```

Update the doc comment above `Remove` to note that an absolute `target` is used
as-is and that removing an already-deleted worktree is a successful no-op.

### Why `filepath.IsAbs` (not a separator check)
The issue offers `IsAbs` or "check for path separators". Callers always pass
absolute paths, so `IsAbs` precisely fixes the bug. A separator check would
misroute legitimate slashed branch names (`feature/x`) that today correctly
resolve via `m.Path`, so `IsAbs` is the lower-regression choice.

## Tests

**File: `internal/worktree/manager_test.go`** — add a regression test alongside
`TestManagerAddListRemove` (same `git`-availability skip + `initRepo` helpers):

- `TestRemoveMissingWorktreeDir`: `Add` a branch, `os.RemoveAll` its worktree
  directory to simulate an already-deleted worktree, then `Remove(path, true)`.
  Assert it returns `nil`; assert the branch is gone from `List()`; and assert no
  mangled sibling directory was created under
  `$HOME/worktrees/<ws>/` (the sanitized-abs-path leaf must not exist).

Existing `TestManagerAddListRemove` already covers branch-name removal and
live-path removal, so those criteria stay guarded.

## Verification
- `go test ./internal/worktree/...` — new and existing tests pass.

toolUseResult.content:
# Fix: `worktree.Manager.Remove` mangles an already-deleted path into a branch

## Context
Issue #134. Deleting a workspace fails when a session's worktree directory has
already been removed from disk. The teardown surfaces a mangled path:

```
error: remove worktree: git worktree remove --force \
  /home/jcarlsson/.wasa/worktrees/4fc7d5c5c1a2/home-jcarlsson-.wasa-worktrees-4fc7d5c5c1a2-task-pink
```

The leaf segment is a sanitized copy of the whole absolute path. The teardown
callers (`internal/cli/finish.go:174`, `internal/launch/launch.go:581`) pass the
**absolute worktree path** to `Remove`. In `Remove`
(`internal/worktree/worktree.go:145`) the path-vs-branch decision uses
`os.Stat`:

```go
path := target
if _, err := os.Stat(target); err != nil { // dir already gone → stat fails
    path = m.Path(target)                   // treats the abs path AS a branch
}
```

When the directory is missing, `os.Stat` fails, so `m.Path` runs `sanitizeBranch`
(`internal/worktree/placement.go:47`) on the absolute path, producing
`$HOME/worktrees/<ws>/<sanitized-whole-path>`. `git worktree remove` on that
bogus path fails and wedges the entire workspace delete.

Intended outcome: absolute paths are never re-sanitized, and an
already-removed worktree makes `Remove` a graceful no-op so workspace deletion
completes.

## Change

**File: `internal/worktree/worktree.go` — rewrite `Remove` (lines 145–161).**

1. **Route by path shape, not by `os.Stat`.** Treat `target` as an existing path
   when `filepath.IsAbs(target)` is true; otherwise resolve it as a branch name
   via `m.Path(target)`. This keeps absolute paths verbatim (fixing the mangle)
   while plain branch names — including slashed names like `feature/x`, which are
   not absolute — still flow through the layout. `filepath` is already imported.

2. **Make removal idempotent.** If `git worktree remove` fails, check the disk:
   when the resolved path no longer exists (`os.IsNotExist`), the worktree is
   already gone — run `git worktree prune` (best-effort, ignore its error) to drop
   stale admin metadata and return `nil`. For any other failure (e.g. a dirty
   worktree removed without `--force`, where the directory still exists), surface
   git's error unchanged.

Sketch:

```go
func (m *Manager) Remove(target string, force bool) error {
    if target == "" {
        return errors.New("target must not be empty")
    }

    path := target
    if !filepath.IsAbs(target) {
        path = m.Path(target)
    }

    args := []string{"worktree", "remove"}
    if force {
        args = append(args, "--force")
    }
    if _, err := m.git(append(args, path)...); err != nil {
        if _, statErr := os.Stat(path); os.IsNotExist(statErr) {
            _, _ = m.git("worktree", "prune") // already gone; drop stale metadata
            return nil
        }
        return err
    }
    return nil
}
```

Update the doc comment above `Remove` to note that an absolute `target` is used
as-is and that removing an already-deleted worktree is a successful no-op.

### Why `filepath.IsAbs` (not a separator check)
The issue offers `IsAbs` or "check for path separators". Callers always pass
absolute paths, so `IsAbs` precisely fixes the bug. A separator check would
misroute legitimate slashed branch names (`feature/x`) that today correctly
resolve via `m.Path`, so `IsAbs` is the lower-regression choice.

## Tests

**File: `internal/worktree/manager_test.go`** — add a regression test alongside
`TestManagerAddListRemove` (same `git`-availability skip + `initRepo` helpers):

- `TestRemoveMissingWorktreeDir`: `Add` a branch, `os.RemoveAll` its worktree
  directory to simulate an already-deleted worktree, then `Remove(path, true)`.
  Assert it returns `nil`; assert the branch is gone from `List()`; and assert no
  mangled sibling directory was created under
  `$HOME/worktrees/<ws>/` (the sanitized-abs-path leaf must not exist).

Existing `TestManagerAddListRemove` already covers branch-name removal and
live-path removal, so those criteria stay guarded.

## Verification
- `go test ./internal/worktree/...` — new and existing tests pass.
- `go build ./...` — compiles.
- End-to-end sanity: create a worktree session, `rm -rf` its worktree directory,
  then delete the workspace / finish the session and confirm teardown succeeds
  with no mangled-path error.

content:
Done. Both tests pass and the build is clean.

## Summary

Fixed issue #134 — `worktree.Manager.Remove` mangling an already-deleted worktree path into a branch.

**`internal/worktree/worktree.go`** — rewrote `Remove`:
- **Routing now uses `filepath.IsAbs(target)`** instead of `os.Stat`. An absolute path is used verbatim; only non-absolute targets are resolved as branch names via `m.Path`. This is the root-cause fix: a missing directory no longer causes the absolute path to be sanitized into a bogus `.../<workspace>/<sanitized-whole-path>` segment.
- **Removal is now idempotent.** If `git worktree remove` fails and the resolved path no longer exists on disk, it best-effort runs `git worktree prune` to drop stale metadata and returns `nil`. Any other failure (e.g. a dirty worktree without `--force`, whose directory still exists) still surfaces git's error unchanged.
- Updated the doc comment to describe both behaviors.

**`internal/worktree/manager_test.go`** — added `TestRemoveMissingWorktreeDir`: adds a worktree, `os.RemoveAll`s its directory to simulate an already-deleted worktree, then asserts `Remove(path, true)` succeeds, the worktree is gone from `List()`, and no mangled sibling directory was created.

Verification: `go test ./internal/worktree/...` passes (new test confirmed running, not skipped) and `go build ./...` is clean.

Note: I used `filepath.IsAbs` alone rather than a separator check, since all teardown callers pass absolute paths and a separator check would misroute legitimate slashed branch names like `feature/x`. This matches the recommendation in the approved plan.
